text: The surgical field shows a midline incisional hernia repair with an ileal conduit visible on the right side.
The surgeon uses an ETEP approach, placing ports in the left abdomen and crossing over to the surgical site.
The surgeon dissects around the stoma, carefully maneuvering around the visible tissue structures.
The surgeon completes the dissection around the stoma, revealing the surrounding tissue and anatomical structures.
The surgeon navigates the right retrorectus space, revealing the anatomical area adjacent to the rectus muscle.
The video shows a measurement of four centimeters between the right edge of the defect and the stoma, highlighting the limited space available for surgical maneuvers.
The surgeon measures the midline defect, which is approximately eight centimeters by eleven centimeters in size.
The video shows a single large hernia, visually identifiable within the surgical field.
The surgeon provides a view of the ileal conduit, highlighting its position relative to the surgical field.
The surgeon sutures the anterior defect, bringing the edges of the tissue together.
The surgeon leaves approximately one centimeter of the posterior rectus sheath intact, noting that the anterior rectus sheath may retract, as observed in this case.
The posterior rectus sheath is visible, providing a clear view of the fascia beneath it.
The surgeon lays out sutures and progressively tensions them to close the anterior defect, using a twenty-four inch OV lock suture.
The surgeon takes small bites of the hernia sac, aiming to reduce the size of the seroma and transform it into a multi-loculated seroma.
The surgeon carefully takes bites of the ipsilateral edge, ensuring that the fascia is visible and avoiding blind grabs of the muscle tissue.
The surgeon peels back the muscle to expose the fascia and ensures the linea alba is encircled.
The surgeon takes solid bites of tissue, likely referring to suturing or grasping techniques during the procedure.
The surgeon progressively tensions the defect, visually drawing the tissue edges closer together.
The surgeon completes the closure of the defect, ensuring the tissue edges are securely approximated.
The surgeon leaves approximately five centimeters of tissue cephalad to ensure a good overlap during the defect closure.
The surgeon runs the suture back along the defect to lock it in place, ensuring the closure is secure.
A parietine mesh measuring nineteen by thirty centimeters is displayed.
The camera provides a view looking down into the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs a lower right one-third transversus abdominis release (TAR) to assist in closing a large posterior gap.
The camera view is oriented cephalad, providing a perspective looking upwards from the pelvis.
The surgeon presses the parietine mesh against the stoma, ensuring it is securely positioned over the surgical site.
The surgeon plans to slit the parietine mesh and wrap it around the stoma, preparing to secure it in place.
The surgeon positions the mesh to achieve more than four centimeters of overlap both cephalad and caudal to the stoma, ensuring adequate coverage.
The surgeon measures approximately four centimeters at the stoma, ensuring adequate mesh overlap.
The surgeon sutures the mesh down to the posterior rectus sheath due to insufficient overlap around the stoma, ensuring secure placement despite the limited mesh coverage.
The surgeon sutures the mesh around the stoma, ensuring it is securely attached due to inadequate overlap.
The surgeon demonstrates how the mesh is tucked beyond the tar point, showing its positioning relative to the surrounding anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon holds a 3-0 Vicryl suture, preparing to use it in the procedure.
The surgeon fixates the mesh at multiple points to prevent shifting, securing it in place over the surgical site.
The surgeon places a 19 French round Jackson-Pratt (JP) drain at the conclusion of the procedure.
